## Graph 1
<img src="{{ site.baseurl }}/static/imgs/a1.png" alt="Analysis 1" width="500"/>

### Analysis
The distribution is skewed toward higher ratings, suggesting many students think pre-lecture videos would be helpful. However, there are still some lower ratings, so support is not unanimous.

## Graph 2
<img src="{{ site.baseurl }}/static/imgs/a2.png" alt="Analysis 2" width="500"/>

### Analysis
This boxplot compares difficulty across different pre_lecture_videos ratings. Higher pre_lecture_videos ratings generally line up with slightly higher reported difficulty, suggesting students who find the course harder may value pre-lecture videos more. However, the boxes overlap, so this is a trend rather than a strong separation.

## Graph 3
<img src="{{ site.baseurl }}/static/imgs/a3.png" alt="Analysis 3" width="500"/>

### Analysis
This boxplot compares would_recommend across different pre_lecture_videos ratings. Most groups have high recommendation scores (often around 5–7) and overlap a lot, so support for pre-lecture videos does not clearly predict whether students would recommend the course.

## Conclusion
Based on the survey data, optional pre-lecture videos look like a promising improvement. The distribution of pre_lecture_videos is skewed toward higher ratings (5–7), suggesting many students would find short preview videos helpful. In the difficulty boxplot, higher interest in pre-lecture videos generally corresponds to slightly higher reported difficulty, but the groups overlap substantially, so this looks like a weak-to-moderate trend rather than a clear split. In the would_recommend boxplot, recommendation scores stay high across most groups with a lot of overlap, so pre-lecture video interest does not strongly predict whether students would recommend the course.

## Recommendation
I recommend adding optional 5–10 minute pre-lecture videos that preview key concepts and one small example each week, positioned as a supplement.

## Trade-offs and future data
The main trade-off is the time required to create and update videos, and there is a risk some students may depend on them instead of attending lecture. If implemented, future data like view counts, student preparedness ratings, and before/after quiz or assignment outcomes would help measure the impact more directly.
